imageCaption: The ruined tower of Morthis, where the first ember still burns.
text:
The tower had not spoken in forty years. That was what they said in Kelver's Crossing — the old women who braided rope by the well, the smiths who worked past dark, the children who were warned away from the ridge when the fog came down low. The tower of Morthis had fallen silent, and the world had quietly agreed to forget it.

Sable Ashveil had not agreed.

She climbed by feel, her lantern shuttered against the wind, one hand on stone that breathed cold through her gloves. The stairs were wrong. She had known they would be wrong — crumbling in places, absent in others — but there was a particular wrongness to the way the darkness pooled at the center of each landing, as though something had been standing there very recently and had only just thought to leave.

At the seventh landing she stopped. The ember was real.
